main_claims.claim: Neutron interactions with lithium in DT fusion breeding blankets can produce dark sector scalars/pseudoscalars via nuclear transitions, generating a detectable flux outside the reactor.
main_claims.notes: The mechanism is physically well-motivated and directly analogous to established fission-reactor axion-search proposals (refs 11–18 in the paper). However, without the full text the reviewer cannot verify the flux estimate, the assumed nuclear matrix elements for relevant Li-7 transitions, or the geometry/shielding treatment.

main_claims.claim: Year-long searches at current and future thermonuclear fusion reactors can set leading constraints on dark scalar- and dark pseudoscalar-nucleon couplings.
main_claims.notes: This is the central quantitative claim. It depends on correctly computed production cross sections, branching ratios to dark sector states, flux attenuation through the blanket and vessel, and detector response. Without access to figures or tables the reviewer cannot confirm the claim stands against competing bounds. The recast of the SNO limit for CP-even scalar is cited as supporting evidence.

main_claims.claim: The SNO axion bound can be recast to constrain a CP-even scalar.
main_claims.notes: The recasting methodology is a recognized technique (see XENON1T/LUX recast in ref 7). The specific recast plausibly involves the solar flux of scalars from 8B or pp-chain nuclear transitions. Cannot verify the kinematics or efficiency assumptions without the full text.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract, refs 65–66 (SNO and Bhusal et al.)

main_claims.claim: Fusion reactors offer a qualitatively different and advantageous probe compared to fission reactors due to the high-intensity, near-monoenergetic neutron flux and the lithium breeding blanket chemistry.
main_claims.notes: The comparative advantage claim (better reach than existing fission-reactor searches) is implicit in the abstract. Cannot evaluate it without the sensitivity plots showing the proposed bounds relative to existing limits.
main_claims.paper_location: Implied throughout abstract; motivation section not accessible

missing_assumptions.assumption: The dominant dark-sector production mode is assumed to occur through specific nuclear transitions in the Li breeding blanket, and the relevant branching ratios (scalar emission vs. photon emission) are treated with certain nuclear structure inputs. The sensitivity of results to the choice of nuclear model or transition form factor is not visible in the abstract.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If the assumed matrix elements are optimistic or if the authors select only the most favorable transition, the projected sensitivity could be overstated by a factor of a few to an order of magnitude.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Attenuation of the dark-sector flux by the reactor vessel, shielding structures, and surrounding materials is assumed to be small or is carefully computed. Fusion reactors have multi-meter steel and blanket structures between the plasma and any external detector.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Dark sector particles are feebly interacting but not entirely non-interacting for all coupling values; photon-coupled or nucleon-coupled scalars with couplings near the detection threshold could still be partially absorbed by thick shielding, reducing the detectable flux.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Existing background rates at proposed detection sites (near ITER or DEMO) are assumed to be manageable for the detection strategies proposed. Fusion reactors produce enormous radiation backgrounds.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The near-detector environment at a fusion reactor is radiation-hard and background-rich. If background subtraction or shielding assumptions are not validated, the projected sensitivity may be unachievable in practice.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The mass range and coupling hierarchy of the exotic scalars are implicitly constrained by the nuclear transition energies (few MeV range). The paper presumably assumes the scalar is lighter than the relevant nuclear level energy. This upper mass cutoff is not stated in the abstract.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The reach is bounded from above by the transition energy and from below by existing laboratory bounds. The accessible window must be carefully justified.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the projected exclusion limits on scalar/pseudoscalar-nucleon couplings actually exceed all existing constraints (stellar cooling, SNO, fission reactor, beam dump) in any mass window, or is the 'leading constraints' claim restricted to a narrow region?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The reviewer does not have access to the figures or tables containing the exclusion plots. Verifying the 'leading constraints' claim requires comparing the paper's projected curves against all cited existing bounds in the same parameter space.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are the nuclear matrix elements for the relevant Li-7 (and 6Li) neutron-capture transitions used in the dark sector emission rate consistent with the range of values in the nuclear structure literature (refs 49–52), and does the paper include uncertainty bands or sensitivity analyses?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The detailed calculation is in the body of the paper which is not accessible. Nuclear matrix element uncertainties are a known quantitative issue that requires reading the actual derivation and comparing against the cited nuclear physics references.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the SNO recast for a CP-even scalar methodologically sound — specifically, does the recast correctly account for the different angular distribution, polarization, and detection cross section of a scalar vs. pseudoscalar in the SNO heavy water detector?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The recast methodology is described in the body of the paper which is inaccessible. CP-even vs. CP-odd scalars have different nucleon matrix elements and different SNO detection signatures; verifying the recast requires reading the detailed derivation.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: What detector concept and location are assumed for the sensitivity projections? Are any experimental group commitments or letters of intent associated with this work, or is it purely a phenomenological proposal?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This is not stated in the abstract. For 'leading constraints' from year-long searches to be meaningful rather than speculative, a realistic detector proposal needs to exist; this cannot be assessed without the full text or supplementary material.

novelty_assessment: The core novelty is genuine: using DT fusion reactors — rather than fission power reactors or solar observations — as a source for light dark sector particle searches. The production environment (high-intensity 14 MeV neutrons thermalized in a Li blanket) and its nuclear transition spectrum differ qualitatively from fission reactor searches, justifying the new proposal. The recast of the SNO bound to a CP-even scalar is an additional, more modest, novel contribution. The general strategy of reactor-based axion/ALP searches is not new (refs 11–18 in the paper), but the specific DT fusion context, with its distinctive neutron flux and lithium breeding chemistry, is a meaningful extension. The overall novelty is moderate to good for a phenomenology paper: the framework is well-established but the application is new and timely given increasing fusion reactor construction activity.
score_rationale: The paper is published in JHEP after peer review, which provides baseline assurance of technical competence. The physics mechanism is well-grounded in established nuclear axion-search methodology applied to a genuinely new environment (DT fusion breeding blankets). Novelty and significance are above average: fusion reactors are a topical and underexplored probe of feebly-coupled particles, and leading constraints in any coupling window would be valuable. However, the reviewer's confidence is substantially curtailed by the absence of the full paper text — no equations, figures, tables, or detailed derivations are available, making it impossible to audit the flux calculation, nuclear matrix element treatment, background assumptions, or comparison with competing bounds. The 'Big Bang Theory' TV episode citations (refs 95–96) are a minor quirk that does not affect the science. The overall score of 6.0 reflects a competent, novel phenomenology paper with good publication venue, discounted by the reviewer's inability to verify its central quantitative claims from the available material.
summary: This paper proposes deuterium-tritium (DT) fusion reactors as a novel source environment for producing and detecting feebly-interacting light scalars and pseudoscalars (dark sector particles). The core mechanism is neutron capture on lithium-7 in the tritium breeding blanket, which triggers nuclear M1 (and other) transitions capable of emitting dark sector particles instead of photons. The authors estimate the resulting flux of dark sector particles outside the reactor wall and compute projected sensitivities for year-long searches at existing and future reactors (ITER, DEMO, ARC). They additionally recast the SNO solar axion bound for a CP-even scalar. The paper is published in JHEP (October 2025). NOTE: The reviewer only has access to the HTML landing page (abstract, references, acknowledgments, author affiliations), not the full text, equations, or figures. This substantially limits the depth of technical review possible.

technical_objections.evidence: References 49 (Heil et al. 1998), 50 (Fernando et al. 2012), 51 (Lynn et al. 1991) are cited — their spread in cross-section values is a known uncertainty in the literature. The paper's reliance on these is visible from the references but the treatment of uncertainty bands is not.
technical_objections.objection: Nuclear matrix element uncertainties for Li-7 neutron-capture transitions are significant and are not acknowledged in the abstract. The dominant M1 and E1 amplitudes for 6Li(n,γ)7Li and 7Li(n,γ)8Li are known to have theoretical uncertainties of order tens of percent (see refs 49–52 the paper cites). If the sensitivity estimate is driven by a single dominant transition whose matrix element is uncertain, the projected bounds could shift substantially.
technical_objections.paper_location: Production rate calculation (section not accessible); refs 49–52

technical_objections.evidence: Refs 80 (Hardy & Lasenby 2017), 81 (Bottaro et al. 2023), 82 (Hardy et al. 2025) are all cited, suggesting the authors are aware of and include these, but the claim cannot be verified.
technical_objections.objection: The paper claims to set 'leading constraints,' but comparison to supernova (SN1987A) and stellar cooling bounds on hadronic scalars/pseudoscalars (refs 80–82: Hardy & Lasenby, Bottaro et al., Hardy et al.) is critical. These astrophysical bounds are often very strong and cover similar mass–coupling parameter space. Without the exclusion plots the reviewer cannot verify that the reactor bounds actually exceed them in any region.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract (leading constraints claim); bounds comparison section not accessible

technical_objections.evidence: Ref [95]: 'S05e01 & s05e02: The skank reflex analysis & the infestation hypothesis'; ref [96]: 'S05e03: The pulled groin extrapolation' — both link to a Big Bang Theory fan blog. Acknowledgments: 'We thank Roni Harnik for pointing us to Big Bang Theory discussion of axion production.'
technical_objections.objection: The Big Bang Theory TV show appears as references 95 and 96 in the reference list (acknowledged by the authors as a source for 'axion production' discussion in the TV show). While the acknowledgment makes the intent clear and this is explicitly playful, it is still unusual to cite a fictional television comedy as a scientific reference in a JHEP paper.

technical_objections.evidence: The bundle contains only the HTML landing page (abstract, references, metadata). No equations, tables, or figures are included.
technical_objections.objection: The full text and figures are not available to this reviewer. Sensitivity estimates for dark sector searches depend sensitively on assumed detector efficiencies, signal-to-background ratios, background models, and systematic uncertainties — none of which can be verified from the abstract alone.
